questions.question: 中文导读：Why does OCR use CTC loss instead of plain cross-entropy?
options: 中文选项：CTC is faster

中文选项：OCR maps a fixed-length feature sequence to a variable-length character sequence without per-timestep alignment; CTC loss marginalises over all alignments that reduce to the target after removing repeats and blanks, so you can train without character-level timing labels
中文选项：CTC is required by PyTorch
中文选项：Cross-entropy overflows for long sequences
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Recognition produces one distribution per time step, but the target text has no per-step alignment. CTC handles that by summing over every alignment that reduces to the target via the merge-repeats-and-remove-blanks operation. This is why CRNNs can be trained on just (image, text) pairs without knowing where each character starts and ends in the image.

questions.question: 中文导读：In CTC decoding, what does the blank token do?
options: 中文选项：Pads the sequence to a fixed length
中文选项：Separates adjacent identical characters so that outputs like 'hello' with a double L can be produced by the model emitting 'h e l _ l o' — without the blank, 'l l' would collapse to one 'l'
中文选项：Masks noisy regions
中文选项：Indicates end of sequence
questions.explanation: 中文解析：The blank is the mechanism that lets CTC encode double characters. 'll' needs a blank between the two l's so the 'merge repeats' step keeps them distinct: 'l _ l' survives merging, 'l l' does not. Without the blank, CTC cannot represent any word containing adjacent identical characters.

questions.question: 中文导读：Why is greedy CTC decoding sometimes better than beam search on simple datasets?
options: 中文选项：Greedy is always better

中文选项：When the model is confident at every step (clean handwriting, printed fonts), greedy's argmax is usually the beam winner; beam search adds latency with no accuracy gain. On noisy or ambiguous inputs, beam search helps
中文选项：Beam search requires a language model
中文选项：Greedy is easier to implement
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Beam search only wins when the per-step argmax is unreliable — ambiguous characters, overlapping letters, degraded scans. For clean printed text, the model is confident at each step and greedy is within 1% CER of beam. Always benchmark both; the latency trade is real.

questions.question: 中文导读：Donut skips explicit text detection and character recognition. How?
options: 中文选项：It uses an external OCR engine internally

中文选项：It is a ViT encoder + text decoder trained to produce the final output string (often JSON) directly from the image; the model learns both localisation and recognition implicitly through the training objective
中文选项：It is limited to document categories with fixed layouts
中文选项：It preprocesses images with a CNN text detector
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Donut is an end-to-end model: the ViT encoder sees the whole document, the transformer decoder emits the target text or JSON auto-regressively. No pipeline of detector + recogniser + layout module. This skips error accumulation across stages but requires large labelled training sets for the specific document types you care about.

questions.question: 中文导读：For extracting `invoice_total` from receipts, which approach is typically best in 2026?
options: 中文选项：A hand-crafted regex on Tesseract output

中文选项：Fine-tuned Donut or a VLM on a small labelled set of the target receipts; both handle layout and semantic extraction in one model and generalise across receipt variants better than pipeline-based approaches
中文选项：Only classical OCR is reliable enough
中文选项：A separate CNN for each field
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Structured-field extraction used to be the domain of LayoutLM + heuristics. End-to-end models (Donut, Qwen-VL-OCR) have overtaken that path: they accept an image and produce the JSON directly. For a small set of labelled receipts (100-1000), fine-tuning Donut reaches higher F1 than any pipeline of OCR + rules.
